description: Adapt lattice sieving techniques from computational number theory (used in the Number Field Sieve for integer factoring) to enumerate supplementary difference sets. The SDS conditions define a lattice of valid block configurations in Z^{4x167}. Short vectors correspond to SDS with small block sizes. Lattice reduction (LLL/BKZ) followed by sieving can systematically enumerate candidate solutions.
mathematical_formalization: Define autocorrelation lattice L = {(x_0,...,x_3) in Z^{4x167} : forall g!=0, sum_i N_{supp(x_i)}(g) = lambda}. This is an affine sublattice of Z^{668}. Apply LLL reduction to find short vectors. Candidate SDS correspond to 0/1 vectors in L. Use CVP to find nearest 0/1 vector to a lattice point.
analogical_connections: Lattice sieving for SDS parallels lattice sieving for smooth values in NFS -- both seek structured elements in high-dimensional lattices
LLL reduction is analogous to Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization -- both find nearly orthogonal bases
The CVP formulation connects Hadamard matrix construction to lattice-based cryptography
implementation_hypothesis: Transform SDS autocorrelation conditions into a system of linear equations modulo suitable values. Construct the associated lattice. Apply BKZ-2.0 reduction. For each reduced basis vector, round to nearest 0/1 vector and check if it forms a valid SDS block. Use Schnorr-Euchner enumeration.
experiment_seed: Construct the autocorrelation lattice for v=23 (known SDS for order 92). Apply LLL and enumerate nearby 0/1 vectors. Verify known SDS solutions are found. Measure lattice dimension and gap. Scale to v=47, then v=167.
